name: Divine order (Ma'at)
shortDescription: Ma'at represents the ancient Egyptian concept of truth, balance, order, law, morality, and justice. It was the fundamental principle that governed the universe and human society.
description: Ma'at was the central concept in ancient Egyptian philosophy and religion, embodying truth, justice, cosmic balance, and order. It represented the ideal state of the universe and the harmonious relationship between gods, humans, and the natural world. Ma'at was not merely a passive state of equilibrium but an active principle that required constant maintenance and adherence. The pharaoh, as the intermediary between the gods and humanity, had the crucial responsibility of upholding Ma'at on Earth. This involved maintaining social justice, defending the kingdom, and performing rituals to ensure the continued stability of the cosmos. Disruptions to Ma'at, such as injustice, chaos, or imbalance, were believed to have dire consequences for both the individual and the community. The weighing of the heart ceremony, depicted in the Book of the Dead, was a crucial judgment in the afterlife, where the deceased's heart was weighed against the feather of Ma'at. If the heart was heavier, burdened by sin and transgression, it would be devoured by Ammit, the Devourer of Souls, preventing the deceased from achieving eternal life. Conversely, a heart in balance with Ma'at ensured passage to the afterlife and union with Osiris. The concept of Ma'at is closely intertwined with other Egyptian deities such as Ra, the sun god, who journeys across the sky each day, upholding the cosmic order; Osiris, ruler of the underworld, who judges souls according to Ma'at's principles; and Isis, who embodies magical power and protection to restore balance when it is disrupted. The ankh, symbolizing eternal life, is also related to Ma'at, as achieving eternal life was dependent on living in accordance with its principles. The Ba, or soul, could only achieve its potential if the deceased lived in harmony with Ma'at.

sources.title: The Egyptian Book of the Dead
sources.author: E.A. Wallis Budge

sources.title: Religion and Magic in Ancient Egypt
sources.author: Rosalie David
